column1: Why is population considered the pivotal element in social studies?
column2: It is the point of reference from which all other elements derive significance and meaning.

column1: How does the textbook define when a natural event like a flood becomes a 'disaster'?
column2: When the event affects a crowded village or town inhabited by human beings.

column1: Human beings are considered both _____ and _____ of the earth's resources.
column2: producers; consumers

column1: What was India's total population recorded as of March 2011?
column2: $1,210.6$ million (or $1.21$ billion).

column1: What percentage of the world's population lived in India in 2011?
column2: More than $17$ per cent.

column1: India's land area of $3.28$ million square km accounts for what percentage of the world's total area?
column2: $2.4$ per cent.

column1: Which Indian state was identified as the most populous in the 2011 Census?
column2: Uttar Pradesh.

column1: According to the 2011 Census, what was the approximate population size of Uttar Pradesh?
column2: $199$ million.

column1: Which Himalayan state had a population of only about $0.6$ million in 2011?
column2: Sikkim.

column1: What was the population of Lakshadweep according to the 2011 Census?
column2: $64,429$ people.

column1: List the five states where almost half of India's population resides.
column2: Uttar Pradesh, Maharashtra, Bihar, West Bengal, and Andhra Pradesh.

column1: What percentage of India's total population resides in Rajasthan, the country's biggest state by area?
column2: $5.5$ per cent.

column1: Term: Census
column2: Definition: An official enumeration of a population done periodically.

column1: In what year was the first population census held in India?
In what year was the first complete census taken in India?
How frequently are censuses held in India?
column2: Every tenth year.

column1: Formula: Population Density
column2: The number of persons per unit area.

column1: Which two countries have higher average population densities than India?
column2: Bangladesh and Japan.

column1: What was the average population density of India in the year 2011?
column2: $382$ persons per sq km.

column1: Which state had the highest population density in India in 2011?
column2: Bihar ($1,102$ persons per sq km).

column1: Which state had the lowest population density in India in 2011?
column2: Arunachal Pradesh ($17$ persons per sq km).

column1: What physical factors contribute to sparse population in areas with densities below $250$ persons per sq km?
column2: Rugged terrain and unfavourable climatic conditions.

column1: Why do the Northern plains and Kerala have very high population densities?
column2: They feature flat plains with fertile soils and abundant rainfall.

column1: How is the 'absolute increase' of a population calculated for a decade?
column2: By subtracting the population of the earlier decade from the population of the later decade.

column1: What is the meaning of an 'annual growth rate' of $2$ per cent?
column2: There was an increase of two persons for every $100$ persons in the base population in a given year.

column1: What was the trend of India's annual population growth rate between 1951 and 1981?
column2: It was steadily increasing.

column1: Why can a declining growth rate still result in a massive absolute increase in India's population?
column2: When a percentage rate is applied to a very large base population, the total number added remains very large.

column1: According to the United Nations, which country did India overtake in 2023 to become the most populous in the world?
column2: China.

column1: What are the three main processes that influence population change?
column2: Births, deaths, and migrations.

column1: How is the 'natural increase' of a population defined?
column2: The difference between birth rates and death rates.

column1: Term: Birth Rate
column2: Definition: The number of live births per thousand persons in a year.

column1: Term: Death Rate
column2: Definition: The number of deaths per thousand persons in a year.

column1: What has been the main cause of the rapid growth of the Indian population historically?
column2: The rapid decline in death rates while birth rates remained high.

column1: What is the difference between internal and international migration?
column2: Internal occurs within a country, while international occurs between countries.

column1: How does internal migration affect a country's total population size?
column2: It does not change the total size, only the distribution of population within the nation.

column1: In India, what is the primary direction of internal migration?
column2: From rural areas to urban areas.

column1: Describe the 'push' factor driving migration from rural areas in India.
column2: Adverse conditions of poverty and unemployment in rural regions.

column1: Describe the 'pull' factor attracting migrants to Indian cities.
column2: Increased employment opportunities and better living conditions.

column1: What percentage of India's total population was urban in 2011?
column2: $31.80$ per cent.

column1: How many 'million plus cities' were there in India in 2011?
column2: $53$ cities.

column1: What age group is classified as the 'adolescent population' in India?
column2: $10$ to $19$ years.

column1: Adolescents constitute what fraction of India's total population?
column2: One-fifth.

column1: What is a major health concern for a large number of adolescent girls in India?
column2: Anaemia.

column1: In what year did the Government of India initiate the comprehensive Family Planning Programme?
What is the primary objective of the National Population Policy (NPP) 2000 regarding education?
column2: Imparting free and compulsory school education up to $14$ years of age.

column1: The NPP 2000 aims to reduce the infant mortality rate to below what level?
column2: $30$ per $1,000$ live births.

column1: According to the Census, what is the definition of a 'literate' person?
column2: A person aged $7$ years and above who can read and write any language with understanding.

column1: Term: Adolescence
column2: Definition: A period in which a person is no longer a child and not yet an adult, typically aged $10$ to $19$ years.

column1: Term: Dependency Ratio
column2: Definition: The ratio of people of dependent age (under $15$ and over $60$) to those of economically active ages ($15$-$59$).

column1: Term: Life Expectancy
column2: Definition: The average number of years a person is expected to live.

column1: Term: Sex-ratio
column2: Definition: The number of females per thousand males in a population.

column1: What type of land is an 'alluvial plain'?
column2: A level tract of land made of fine rock material brought down by a river.

column1: What is a 'million plus city' in the context of Indian demographics?
column2: A city with a population of more than one million or $10$ lakh.

column1: Migration: What is the difference between immigration and emigration?
column2: Immigration is coming to a country from another; emigration is leaving one's country for another.

column1: How is the 'growth rate of population' estimated?
column2: By comparing the absolute increase in population with the base population.

column1: What geographical area is referred to by the term 'Indian mainland'?
column2: The contiguous landmass from Jammu and Kashmir to Kanniyakumari and Gujarat to Arunachal Pradesh.

column1: Term: Indian Standard Time
column2: Definition: The local time along the Standard Meridian of India ($82^{\circ}30'E$).

column1: Which states in India generally have moderate population densities?
column2: Assam and most of the Peninsular states.

column1: Which state became the $29$th State of India in June 2014?
column2: Telangana.

column1: What occurred on 05.08.19 regarding the state of Jammu and Kashmir?
column2: It was bifurcated into two union territories: Jammu and Kashmir, and Ladakh.

column1: According to Table 6.1, in which decade did India experience its highest annual growth rate?
column2: 1981 (at $2.22$ per cent).

column1: Why is migration considered a determinant factor of population change?
column2: It changes population size and composition (age/sex) in both the area of departure and arrival.

column1: What are 'million plus cities' called in some contexts within the text?
column2: Cities with a population of more than $10$ lakh.

column1: NPP 2000 seeks to achieve universal _____ of children against all vaccine-preventable diseases.
column2: immunisation

column1: What social practice does the NPP 2000 promote to improve the welfare of girls?
column2: Delayed marriage.
